title: practical english usage - Part 329
content:
Can you work with Sophie instead of me today, please?

vocabulary •  503 just You're just beautiful.  I just love your dress.               -> 'there's no other word for it'.

Just can emphasise other words and expressions, with the sense of 'simply', d  emphasiser She's just as bad-tempered as her father.

Thanks. That's just what I wanted.

'What's the time? 'It's just four o'clock.' Just often means 'exactly'.

Could I just use your phone for a moment?

Can/Could I just...? can make a request seem less demanding.

There was only just enough light to read by.

This meaning can be emphasised by only.

We just caught the train.

I just want somebody to love me - that's all.

Complete set of garden tools for just £15.99!

Just can mean 'only', 'nothing more than', 'scarcely'.

b  'only', 'scarcely' I saw him  just after lunch. (= ... very soon after lunch.) the time in question.

In expressions like just after, just before and just when, just suggests closeness to Sam's still around. I saw him  just last week. (= as recently as) Alice has just phoned. (= a short time ago) I'll be down in ɑ  minute - I'm just changing my shirt. (= right now) Just often emphasises the idea of 'at this moment' or 'close to the present'.

just has several meanings.

503 just                                   _______ ► journey,        travel,      trip    and   voyage        612 For other structures in which  a past tense has a present or future meaning, ►    46.

It's high time you got a job.

something is urgent.

The  expression It's high time... is often used in this structure, to say that I'm getting tired. It's time we went home.

It's time she was in bed. It's time you washed those trousers.

The  meaning is present.

It's time can also be followed by a subject with a past tense verb.

2 followed       by   past    tense     with    present      or  future     reference Word  Problems from A  to Z     Section 31 vocabulary •  504  know not     i We've known each other since 1994. (     W   g know each other since 1994.) or something. (►  52 for more details.) A  present perfect tense is used to say how  long one has known somebody not I know exactly what you mean, (     I am knowing. ..) Know cannot usually be used in progressive forms (►  4).

I've never known it (to) rain like this.

infinitive; an infinitive without to is possible in British English.

Know means ’ experience’  in the common structure I've never known + object + They knew that he was a dangerous criminal.

In a less formal style, t/zɑt-elauses are more usual.

He was known to be a dangerous criminal.

The  passive equivalent is quite common in a formal style.

They knew him  to be a dangerous criminal.

In a formal style, know is sometimes followed by object + infinitive.

2 object     + infinitive not I know how to make Spanish omelettes, (     / know to make . ..) Know is not followed directly by infinitives. We   use know how to (►   111).

1  know       how     + infinitive 504 know ►    592 kind     of,   sort     of  and   type     of l('ve) just now realised what I need to do.

in mid-position (►   200) with the verb, with a present perfect or past tense.

I telephoned Anna just now.

in end-position, usually with a past tense.

When  just now means ’ a few moments ago', two positions are possible: I saw Jake just now. He wanted to talk to you.

She’s not in just now. Can I take a message?

Just now can mean either ’ at this moment' or ’ a few moments ago’ . Compare: 3 just     now Alice (has) just left.  I just had a brilliant idea.

'Where's Sam? 'Hejust went out.’ In American English a past tense is also very common.

jaek('s) just called. His wife's had a baby. (The news is the baby, not the I've just had a brilliant idea.

giving news. Compare: possible in British English. A  present perfect is preferred when we  are When  just means ’ a moment ago', past and present perfect tenses are both 2 tenses vocabulary •  505  last, the last, the latest June 16th to July 15th; the last year is the 12 months starting in July 2015.

moment of speaking. On July 15th 2016, the last month is the period from The last week, month, etc is the period of seven/thirty, etc days up to the If ɪ  am speaking in July, last month was June; in 2016, last year was 2015.
